item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't believe everything needs an elaboration or an explanation. Some aspects might, as this is one of my first articles and thus has aged more, but I don't think I need to explain the latter two aspects at all.

Nonetheless, thank you for your feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Didn’t feel fun or out of the ordinary. In the end, I don’t understand if whoever stayed did so voluntarily or not.

Didn’t elaborate on the entity either, and its purpose is completely alien to me.

No explanations on the house or its origins and how they connect to everything else.

No explanation on the places chosen to form part of the family. Not sure if they’re meant as some obscure reference to something.

Until these issues are sorted out, I shall downvote as of the 19th of August, 2019.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I missing something, or were two -1 instances removed between initial containment and the logs?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed ^.^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
“Observar o que você estão dizendo!“

This isn't correct at all. "Olha o que você diz!" would be better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone else who grew up in "West Portugal" this was pretty cool to read. Reminded me pleasantly of irl family gatherings with the group's friendly nosiness and random bouts of Portuguese :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haha, thank you so much <3 This is making me smile big-time, especially since it's the only SCP I've written that I'm not a fan of since writing it. I wanna rewrite it but still keep the same sort of grace and personal feeling. In the meantime though, I'm super appreciative of you loving it :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP has always affected me in a big way. You've told a story set in a terrible reality and infused it with a great deal of heart, you've told a type of personal story that is very difficult to properly tell and you've done it with grace and aplomb. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree that making it less compulsive would be for the better, but overall I like how it's done: it shows that these people legitimately want to better themselves and welcome others, even if they are not always as good as they cpuld be. The fact that it has these specific trigger aspects makes me wonder what started this, which is a plus for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels like it was a lot longer than it really needed to be, and since the effect / overall story wasn't nearly special enough to make up for that, I'm going to downvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will have to disagree. Even though they are not hard to contain, they can still present as a risk to the containment measures. Nobody knows if they might hold other properties or if they might manifest in a way that breaks containment. I had the same problem with SCP-2595 and ended up going for Euclid for the anomalous community, even though they are the friendliest things (while having huge egos) you can encounter.

From the Object Classes Hub:

In particular, any anomaly that exhibits autonomy, sentience and/or sapience is generally classified a Euclid-class entity at minimum, due to the inherent unpredictability of an object that can act or think on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah. They can be easily contained as far as we know, and since they're confined to the house and containing the house is simple and easy and we can do it with minimal oversight, it's safe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since this has sapient entities, shouldn't it be elucidate rather than safe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate compulsion effects as much as anybody, for the reasons you lay out (that and they are lazy and cheaty). But I liked this article because, well, it doesn't seem like an actual compulsion effect. The pattern laid out by which the subject is compelled to return after X days, etc etc was made though observation of one or at most two cases.

The way the D-class subject describes it doesn't sound like a person under "they get you and you become one of them" compulsion, it sounds like someone who had a positive religious experience, and wanted to stay. The Foundation describing it as a compulsion seemed, to me, to be an obvious case of the Foundation not understanding what was going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This suffers from the same issue that plagues a lot of articles that feature compulsion effects, that issue being that the compulsion serves as a shortcut that ultimately leaves the article weaker. Instead of doing the necessary writing to have a character react a certain way, compulsion effects, especially as presented here, wipe away the complex manner in which humans process information and make decisions, and instead puts characters on a set path, independent of any sort of motivation or personality. This piece is relying in great part on the attempt to make these characters seem real on some level, but the step-by-step "this person will express interest in visiting again" etc. compulsion undercuts this effort.

I'm also unsure, thematically, what the Brazilian connection is about. This phenomenon is tied pretty closely to the environs of Martha's Vineyard, but I can't see why someone being of Brazilian descent would matter that centrally in this context. As a result, it feels kind of dissonant to the rest of the narrative.

It's not a bad article, but I'm also fairly unmoved by this, and I get the impression this is supposed to be something that elicits a degree of personal involvement by the reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ding ding, I'm glad you caught it all!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad at all. Well-written, solid, slightly classic, worth an upvote. I will say that the final log didn't quite have the impact I was anticipating, but I really like how, while this turns people into one of the 'family', the D-class didn't seem to be overtly changed as a person. Like, she answered to 'daughter', but otherwise wasn't any different personality-wise than she seemed in the previous log. Any changes don't seem to be outside the bounds of "had a religious experience", and that's kind of powerful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice story, though there were a few cliches. I found a little grammar issue in one of the logs, fixed it. I upvoted by the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the comments everyone. Although…

Sorta spoilers
– hide block

This isn't really a straight-up "thing what converts psople", but rafher, how the Foundation percieves it to be. Most people I showed it to got that, but I'm thinking maybe I'm showing more of the Foundation's point of view than I realized…

Also yes, the D-class is trans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi! I like your article, specially because it includes my nationality and this is quite rare. That given, you do have some automated translation errors that i will be happy to correct for you.

Papai, mamãe, nós não sabemos onde ele vive!

I think you were going for: "Mom, dad, we don't know where he comes from!"
In this case, it would be: "Papai, mamãe, nós não sabemos de onde ele vem!"

SCP-2110-1-E: Mamãe, que está indo sobre?

I think you were going for: "What are you talking about?"
In this case, in Portuguese, it would be: "Mamãe, do que vocês estão falando?"

Even if you weren't going for one of these phrases, just tell me what you meant them to mean and i will tell you the correct phrase in Brazilian Portuguese. They sound very weird.

Besides the two mistakes, i like it and i like the concept, so you have my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An interesting concept, but somewhat unoriginal. +1 anyways, for the interviews.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this article, and echo the question. Is the D-class a transwoman or is she cisgendered?

item.title: Cool
item.content:encoded: The interviews are really interesting, about how they think they're a normal family +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really just seems like a retread of several existing articles. There's nothing here to make me care specifically for this iteration of "anomalous building and when you enter it, you become one."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not too keen on this. At its core, its a thing what converts you into the same thing. You try to inject some spirituality and emotion into the article through the interviews, but it didn't really elicit anything in me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this one. Quick question, is D-3950 a trans woman, or was I reading this wrong?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this is the sort of thing I've seen often. Person goes in, person becomes another clerk/family member/worker at the amusement park/etc. I also found the big argument in the family to be confusing. If it caused all the instances to become emotional, how is it superfluous?

item.title: Author's Note
item.content:encoded:
This image is borrowed from:

Original Source: Wikipedia
Creator: Le grand Cricri

Thanks to Dr Reach, DevFox, Athena_Grey, Sound Chaser, TwistedGears, and others I'm probably forgetting at the moment for looking over and reviewing this skip of mine for the past month or so, and putting up with me. <3

My first ever skip! This has been a month in the making, and inspired by walks in Oak Bluffs, and a recent growing interest in religion, as well as music I mostly listened to while making this.
